Calibration

  1. Optional: use Calibrate Lat (3) and click:
    1) 90°N / North Pole
    2) 0° / Equator
    3) -90° / South edge
  2. Then use Calibrate Long and click a point that lies on the 0° prime meridian direction.
  3. After that, move the mouse to read calibrated latitude/longitude.
  4. Use Measure like Google Earth: click to start, keep clicking to extend the path, and move the mouse to see a live elastic preview line. Press ESC to clear points.

Wind uses live Open-Meteo GFS pressure levels in km/h. Direction label shows where wind comes FROM; arrow shows movement. Select GFS on Windy for closest comparison.

About Map of Truth

Map of Truth

Version 1.3
An interactive Azimuthal Equidistant (north-pole) map visualisation and measurement tool. Designed for educational exploration of different geometric distance models, live celestial events (Sun, Moon, planets, stars, ISS), and global cartographic study.

What's New in V1.3

  • WebView compatibility pass — removed the Android-version / DPR / core-count auto-downgrade. Visual quality is now full by default on every device. A manual Performance Mode switch lives in Layers; it only changes when you flip it.
  • Feature-detection fallbacks — `backdrop-filter`, `:has()`, `env()`, and `transform: translateZ(0)` are probed via `CSS.supports`. Unsupported features get narrow CSS fallbacks (solid translucent panels) instead of a global quality drop.
  • Universal safe DPR cap — every device renders at min(devicePixelRatio, 2.0). Performance Mode lowers the cap to 1.5; nothing ever drops below 1.0.
  • WebView diagnostics — one-time `console.info` log on boot with userAgent, Android version, DPR, viewer size, feature support and performance-mode state for triage from logcat.
  • Try/catch around optional layers — a failed render step (e.g. WebView quirk in radial gradient) no longer kills the whole app; the self-healing loop resumes on the next frame.

What's New in V1.2

  • Ursa Major (الدب الأكبر) constellation — the seven stars of the Big Dipper added to the live celestial sphere with accurate RA/Dec coordinates.
  • 3D planet names now respect the active language (Arabic / English) — Mercury → عطارد, Jupiter → المشتري, etc.
  • Cleaner latitude / longitude labels — shortened to just the coordinate value (23.44°N, 0°, 23.44°S) with stroke-on-fill text and no shadow boxes. Looks correct in both English and Arabic.
  • Smoother Sun/Moon scrub — input-reflow throttled to 5 Hz during drag eliminates first-revolution stutter on mid-range devices.
  • Disabling Day/Night also stops playback — fixes the "map stops refreshing on zoom after scrub" issue.
  • Performance scaling — manual Performance Mode toggle in Layers replaces the older auto-downgrade. Detail level is your choice.
  • Geographic mode pre-rendered — satellite texture loads at 600 ms (was 3 s), and the AE-projection cache rebuilds eagerly the moment any HQ tier arrives.
  • Arabic language — full UI translation (Tahoma, RTL) covering tabs, layers, search & weather panels, measurement methods, planet and constellation names. Switch from Layers → Language.
  • 3D celestial view — tilted sky-dome with elevated Sun, Moon, planets, and zodiac constellations. Drag the Sun/Moon horizontally to scrub time forwards or backwards.
  • Truth (BETA) scale — law of cosines on the AE-flat right-angle triangle. Equator full loop = 40 003 km, Capricorn full loop ≈ 50 448 km.
  • Gleason Scale — straight from Gleason's 1892 longitude diagram (radial-miles table + circumference = 360 × r).
  • Sun/Moon time-scrub gesture — touch and drag to scrub time. Fling for fast forward/reverse playback that decays back to ±1×.
  • Pan-locked at zoomed-out — empty space around the disc becomes a time-scrub surface when the whole globe is visible.
  • Ring rotation in Pan mode only — Measure / Pin clicks on the south rim now hit-test for their primary action.
  • 3D off → clean 2D — exiting the 3D view automatically clears Day/Night, Planets, and Constellations.
  • Self-healing render loop — quality-refresh after intense Sun/Moon scrub no longer stalls.
  • Polished mobile UI — icon-based tabs, single-line status bar, scroll-reset on sheet collapse, locked map centring.

What This App Does

  • Displays the world on a flat-polar Azimuthal Equidistant projection with multiple visual layers (white political, geographic, satellite imagery).
  • Lets you measure distances and routes using five different geometric models (Great Circle, Gleason, Gleason 2, Dome, Truth).
  • Renders live solar terminator (day & night), real-time Moon position & phase, planet positions, the 12 zodiac constellations, and the live ISS orbit.
  • Pin and label arbitrary points; search global cities; show your own GPS location on the map.

Permissions Used

This app requests only the permissions needed for features you choose to use. You can deny or revoke optional permissions at any time in your device settings.

  • Location (precise & coarse) - optional and used only when you enable "My location". Coordinates are processed on device to draw your marker, are not stored by the app, and are not sent to the developer.
  • Internet - used to load requested map tiles, ISS position, city search, and weather or wind data. Network calls are made only for features you open or enable.
  • Storage - browser or WebView local storage is used only for app preferences such as selected layers, units, and panel positions.

Google Play Data Safety Summary

  • No account or profile. The app has no sign-in, account creation, user profile, chat, or user-generated content.
  • No ads, analytics, trackers, or advertising SDKs. The app does not collect advertising IDs and does not sell or share user data for advertising.
  • No developer collection of personal data. The developer does not collect, store, sell, or share names, emails, contacts, photos, device identifiers, or location history.
  • Location is optional. If enabled, it is used only for the live on-map marker and remains on device. The app continues to work when location permission is denied.
  • Local preferences only. Settings saved in local storage can be cleared by clearing app or browser storage.
  • Third-party data requests. Map, weather, wind, geocoding, and ISS features contact the providers listed below directly from the device. Those providers may receive normal request metadata such as IP address, user agent, and requested tile or API parameters under their own privacy policies.

Third-Party Data Sources & Attribution

  • ESRI / ArcGIS Online - World Imagery for satellite mode. Esri and contributor trademarks remain their property.
  • NASA Blue Marble / GSFC - fallback global imagery and educational Earth reference imagery. Public domain where applicable.
  • Natural Earth - coastline and country-boundary vector data. Public domain.
  • Open-Notify / Where The ISS At? - live International Space Station orbital position from public APIs.
  • Open-Meteo - weather, wind, and atmospheric data when weather features are enabled.
  • City names & geocoding - derived from open public datasets and embedded or queried only to support search and map labels.

Third-party data is used only to render requested map and educational features. All trademarks, logos, and brand names remain the property of their respective owners.

Distance Models

Great Circle — shortest spherical path on a globe; appears curved on a flat map.

Gleason — flat-radial; straight lines drawn directly on the projection.

Gleason 2 — hybrid with smooth angular interpolation between flat and curved behaviour.

Dome — arc-based interpolation across an assumed curved surface above the projection.

Truth (BETA) — law of cosines on the AE-flat right-angle triangle. The meridian leg (|Δlat| × 60 nm) and parallel leg (|Δlon| × rmiles, with rmiles(L) = (90°−L) × ⅔) cross at right angles on the AE map, so the law of cosines collapses to c² = a² + b². Anchors: Equator full loop ≈ 40 003 km, Capricorn full loop ≈ 50 448 km, Pole→Equator = 10 001 km.

Each model produces its own valid distances within its own internal geometry. Comparing them is the point of this tool.

Disclaimer & Terms of Use

  • This application is provided strictly for educational, exploratory, and visualisation purposes.
  • It must NOT be used for aviation, maritime, surveying, engineering, emergency response, or any safety- or life-critical decision. Use a certified navigation system for those purposes.
  • Distances, routes, locations, satellite positions, weather data and similar values are approximations drawn from public datasets. Accuracy is not guaranteed.
  • Map projections and distance models in this app describe different geometric assumptions; results depend on the model chosen and are not a statement of physical reality.
  • The app is provided "AS IS", without warranty of any kind, express or implied, including but not limited to the warranties of merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, and non-infringement.
  • To the maximum extent permitted by law, the developer shall not be liable for any claim, damages, loss, or other liability arising from the use of, or inability to use, this application or its data.
  • Features, calculations, data sources and visual styles may change without notice.
  • By using this application you acknowledge and accept the terms above.

Age Rating

Suitable for all audiences. The app contains no advertising, no in-app purchases, no user-generated content, no chat, and no links to external user-controlled content.

Open Source & Credits

Built with vanilla HTML5 Canvas and JavaScript — no third-party frameworks. Astronomical formulas adapted from standard public-domain references (Meeus, NOAA Solar Position Algorithm).
